facts.t: Nineteen hijackers carried out the September 11 terrorist attacks on New York and Washington.
Wikipedia, the free online encyclopedia, was launched on January 15, 2001.
Apple unveiled the first iPod, kicking off the digital music revolution.
George W. Bush was sworn in as the 43rd president of the United States.
Letters laced with anthrax spores were mailed to news outlets and senators, killing five people.
Congress passed the Homeland Security Act, creating the Department of Homeland Security.
Salt Lake City hosted the Winter Olympics amid tight post-9/11 security.
Boston Beer Company released Samuel Adams Utopias, one of the strongest beers ever brewed in America.
Fourteen-year-old Elizabeth Smart was abducted from her Salt Lake City home, sparking a nationwide search.
American Idol premiered on Fox, launching a reality-TV singing dynasty.
U.S.-led forces invaded Iraq, toppling Saddam Hussein's government.
Space Shuttle Columbia broke apart on reentry, killing all seven astronauts aboard.
California voters recalled Governor Gray Davis and elected Arnold Schwarzenegger in his place.
Scientists announced the Human Genome Project had successfully mapped human DNA.
The social networking site Myspace launched, becoming an early internet phenomenon.
George W. Bush won reelection, defeating Senator John Kerry.
Mark Zuckerberg launched Facebook from his Harvard dorm room.
The Boston Red Sox swept the World Series, ending an 86-year championship drought.
Former President Ronald Reagan died at age 93, prompting a week of national mourning.
The documentary Super Size Me put America's fast-food habits under the microscope.
Hurricane Katrina devastated New Orleans and the Gulf Coast, killing over 1,800 people.
Three former PayPal employees founded the video-sharing site YouTube in San Mateo, California.
Former FBI official Mark Felt revealed himself as Watergate's secret source, Deep Throat.
Microsoft's Xbox 360 hit shelves, kicking off the next console generation.
Space Shuttle Discovery flew NASA's first Return to Flight mission after the Columbia disaster.
Google bought YouTube for $1.65 billion, its largest acquisition at the time.
Jack Dorsey and cofounders launched Twitter, a new way to share short public updates.
Nintendo released the motion-controlled Wii console, a surprise smash hit.
Facebook dropped its college-only requirement and opened registration to everyone.
Former President Gerald Ford died at age 93, the longest-lived president up to that time.
Apple released the first iPhone, redefining the smartphone.
Netflix launched a streaming video service, moving beyond its DVD-by-mail roots.
Amazon released the Kindle, its first dedicated e-reader.
J.K. Rowling published the final Harry Potter novel, Deathly Hallows, to record sales.
Two Bear Stearns hedge funds collapsed, an early warning sign of the coming financial crisis.
Barack Obama was elected the first Black president of the United States.
Lehman Brothers collapsed into bankruptcy, deepening the global financial crisis.
Swimmer Michael Phelps won a record eight gold medals at the Beijing Olympics.
Belgium's InBev acquired Anheuser-Busch, forming brewing giant Anheuser-Busch InBev.
Apple launched the App Store, opening the door to a whole new software economy.
Barack Obama was inaugurated as the 44th president of the United States.
Captain Chesley Sullenberger safely landed US Airways Flight 1549 on the Hudson River.
Pop icon Michael Jackson died suddenly at age 50 in Los Angeles.
The mysterious Satoshi Nakamoto mined Bitcoin's genesis block, launching the cryptocurrency.
A Colorado family's claim that a boy had floated away in a homemade balloon turned out to be a hoax.
President Obama signed the Affordable Care Act into law, overhauling U.S. health insurance.
The Deepwater Horizon rig exploded, causing the largest oil spill in U.S. history.
Apple released the first iPad, creating a new category of consumer tablet.
Instagram launched, letting users share filtered photos from their phones.
WikiLeaks published a massive trove of leaked U.S. diplomatic cables.
U.S. Navy SEALs killed Osama bin Laden in a raid on his Pakistan compound.
Apple cofounder Steve Jobs died after a long battle with cancer.
Space Shuttle Atlantis flew NASA's final shuttle mission, ending the 30-year program.
Occupy Wall Street protesters camped in New York's Zuccotti Park to decry income inequality.
Casey Anthony was acquitted of murdering her daughter in a trial that gripped the nation.
Barack Obama won a second term, defeating Republican challenger Mitt Romney.
Hurricane Sandy slammed the East Coast, flooding much of Lower Manhattan.
Colorado became the first U.S. state to legalize recreational marijuana by popular vote.
A gunman killed 20 children and 6 adults at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Connecticut.
Felix Baumgartner jumped from the stratosphere and broke the sound barrier in freefall.
Two bombs exploded near the Boston Marathon finish line, killing three and injuring hundreds.
NSA contractor Edward Snowden leaked classified documents exposing mass government surveillance.
The Supreme Court struck down the Defense of Marriage Act in United States v. Windsor.
A budget standoff shut down the federal government for 16 days.
Twitter went public on the New York Stock Exchange in one of the year's biggest IPOs.
The police killing of Michael Brown in Ferguson, Missouri, ignited nationwide protests.
Colorado's first legal recreational marijuana stores opened their doors on January 1.
The Ice Bucket Challenge went viral, raising tens of millions of dollars for ALS research.
The first case of Ebola diagnosed on U.S. soil was confirmed in Dallas, Texas.
Yankees legend Derek Jeter played the final season of his storied career.
The Supreme Court legalized same-sex marriage nationwide in Obergefell v. Hodges.
The U.S. and Cuba restored diplomatic relations for the first time in over 50 years.
South Carolina removed the Confederate battle flag from its statehouse grounds.
Patriots quarterback Tom Brady was suspended amid the NFL's Deflategate scandal.
U.S. regulators caught Volkswagen cheating emissions tests in the Dieselgate scandal.
Donald Trump was elected the 45th president in a stunning upset over Hillary Clinton.
The Chicago Cubs won the World Series, ending a 108-year championship drought.
The augmented-reality game Pokemon Go became a summer-long cultural craze.
Music icon Prince died suddenly at his Paisley Park estate in Minnesota.
A gorilla named Harambe was shot after a child fell into his enclosure, sparking a viral meme.
Donald Trump was inaugurated as the 45th president of the United States.
A total solar eclipse crossed the entire continental U.S. for the first time since 1918.
Hurricane Harvey dumped record rainfall on Houston, causing catastrophic flooding.
The Me Too movement went viral, sparking a national reckoning over sexual misconduct.
Bitcoin's price rocketed toward $20,000, capping a historic year-end rally.
A mass shooting at a Parkland, Florida high school sparked the March for Our Lives movement.
American actress Meghan Markle married Prince Harry in a royal wedding watched worldwide.
The U.S. men's soccer team failed to qualify for the World Cup for the first time since 1986.
The Facebook-Cambridge Analytica data scandal exposed the misuse of millions of users' data.
Soul legend Aretha Franklin, the Queen of Soul, died at age 76.
The House of Representatives impeached President Trump for the first time.
Disney launched its Disney+ streaming service, entering the crowded streaming wars.
The U.S. marked the 50th anniversary of the Apollo 11 moon landing.
Boeing's 737 MAX fleet was grounded worldwide after two fatal crashes.
Nike pulled a sneaker featuring the Betsy Ross flag after backlash over its historical ties.
The COVID-19 pandemic swept the country, triggering lockdowns and mass disruption.
The murder of George Floyd by police sparked nationwide protests against racial injustice.
Joe Biden was elected the 46th president of the United States.
NBA legend Kobe Bryant died in a helicopter crash along with his daughter and seven others.
Distilleries across the country pivoted to making hand sanitizer amid pandemic shortages.
A mob of Trump supporters stormed the U.S. Capitol to disrupt certification of the election.
Joe Biden was sworn in as the 46th president of the United States.
Amateur traders on Reddit's WallStreetBets triggered a historic short squeeze in GameStop stock.
The container ship Ever Given blocked the Suez Canal for six days, snarling global trade.
The Tokyo Olympics finally opened after a one-year pandemic postponement.
The Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization.
Russia's invasion of Ukraine prompted sweeping U.S. sanctions and global market turmoil.
Elon Musk completed his $44 billion acquisition of Twitter.
Tennis great Serena Williams announced her retirement from professional competition.
NASA's James Webb Space Telescope released its first stunning full-color images.
Silicon Valley Bank collapsed, one of the largest bank failures in U.S. history.
ChatGPT and generative AI chatbots went mainstream, reshaping tech and daily life.
Taylor Swift's Eras Tour became a record-shattering cultural and economic phenomenon.
Barbie and Oppenheimer both opened the same day, sparking the Barbenheimer craze.
Bud Light faced a major consumer boycott after a marketing partnership sparked backlash.
Donald Trump was elected president again, becoming only the second to serve non-consecutive terms.
A total solar eclipse swept from Texas to Maine, visible to tens of millions of Americans.
Caitlin Clark broke the NCAA Division I scoring record in women's college basketball.
Former President Jimmy Carter died at 100, the longest-lived U.S. president in history.
President Biden withdrew from the race in July, endorsing Kamala Harris as the nominee.
Donald Trump was inaugurated for a second, non-consecutive term as U.S. president.
Cardinal Robert Prevost was elected Pope Leo XIV, becoming the first American-born pope in history.
Wildfires tore through the Palisades area of Los Angeles, destroying thousands of homes.
A driver plowed a truck into a New Year's crowd on Bourbon Street in New Orleans, killing over a dozen.
President Trump announced sweeping new tariffs on trading partners, rattling global markets.
